---
title: "Audio Structure Analysis: 'Op de block' by Kevin & Emms"
author: "Mouad Kasmi"
format: 
  dashboard:
    nav-buttons: [github]
    github: https://github.com/FTHeve/hw-musicology
    theme: darkly
    scrolling: true
---

# Overview

## Row {height=100%}

### Column {width=65%}

#### About This Project
I worked on "Op de block" by Kevin and Emms for this assignment. Main question for me was whether the beat, harmony and rhythm actually change a lot or if most of the structure comes from the vocals and small arrangement tweaks.

It is pretty typical hip-hop in that sense: the beat loops for long stretches. When the track still feels like it moves, that is mostly from switching between rappers/singers and little extras in the mix, not from rewriting the whole instrumental every section.

### Column {width=35%}

::: {.card title="Tools used"}

| Step | Tool |
|------|------|
| Audio analysis | R / RStudio |
| Data processing| R |
| Plots | R / ggplot2 |
| Dashboard | Quarto |

:::

# Harmonic Analysis

## Row {height=60%}

### Column

#### Chromagram: Pitch Features Over Time
![Chromagram](graph3.png)

## Row {height=40%}

### Column

#### Key and Chords: How it Feels
The chromagram basically stays stuck on D, F# and A, which fits a D major chord. So harmonically it is not busy.

What stands out is more the rhythm of those hits than a long melodic line. They function a bit like a stab pattern in the beat. That gives a fixed harmonic frame while Kevin and Emms do their thing on top. I did not see big key changes; it is mostly the same loop coming back.


# Structural Analysis

## Row {height=60%}

### Column

#### Self-Similarity (Cosine Distance)
![SSM Cosine](graph2.png)

### Column

#### Self-Similarity (Manhattan Distance)
![SSM Manhattan](graph1.png)

## Row {height=40%}

### Column

#### Building the Song
Cosine and Manhattan self-similarity matrices both pick up repetition in the backing. The cosine one is more about the pitch/chroma pattern repeating, so you get those diagonal stripes when the same harmonic content returns.

Manhattan is a bit sharper on texture changes. Even when the chords are basically the same, you can still see blocks where the overall sound shifts (for example verse vs hook) because of vocals and what is happening in the high frequencies. Underneath, the beat loop is still the same idea.


# Rhythm Analysis

## Row {height=60%}

### Column

#### Tempogram
![Tempogram of Op de block](tempogram.png)

## Row {height=40%}

### Column

#### Tempo (tempogram)
The tempogram for this track is boring in a good way: one strong horizontal band, so the estimated BPM barely drifts. Loud sections do not mean a faster grid here.

That matches what I expected from the instrumental loop. If the beat is quantized and stable, the tempogram should look locked. The interesting stuff in this song is mostly energy and arrangement, not tempo tricks.


# Corpus Clustering

## Row {height=100%}

### Column {width=65%}

#### Playlist Song Clusters (194 Tracks)
![Song Clusters](dendrogram.png)

### Column {width=35%}

#### How the Songs Group Together
I clustered a long class playlist (about 194 tracks) in R using a few Spotify audio features: danceability, energy, valence, acousticness, instrumentalness. Ward linkage on scaled Euclidean distance, then plotted the dendrogram.

Short branches = tracks that score similar on those measures. Higher merges split bigger chunks of the playlist apart. Interpreting genre from that is not perfect (it is only five numbers per song), but you can at least see clumps of "similar vibe" instead of reading 200 titles one by one.
